redFlags: Focal neurology, meningism, persistent reduced GCS, severe agitation with risk, hypoglycaemia, sepsis physiology

fluids/oxygen as needed

keep patient safe and reorientate

leadPattern: Superficial soft tissue infection unless deep/necrotising features present

leadPattern: Split acute septic-obstructive biliary disease from gallbladder inflammation, duct obstruction, and chronic cholestatic liver disease

persistent RUQ pain and Murphy-sign tenderness point more toward cholecystitis than simple biliary colic
obstructive or cholestatic picture, including dark urine or pale stools
brief recurrent post-prandial attacks with well intervals point more toward biliary colic
septic physiology or confusion on top of a biliary pattern
known gallstones, prior biliary colic, or worsening obstructive symptoms
featuresAgainstLead: isolated post-prandial pain settling between attacks without fever or jaundice
Murphy-sign dominant RUQ tenderness without jaundice, which may fit cholecystitis better
chronic itch, fatigue, sicca symptoms, or IBD context without acute sepsis
worstCaseToExclude: Septic biliary obstruction needing urgent source control
Gallstone pancreatitis if severe epigastric pain radiates to the back
redFlags: hypotension, confusion, or other sepsis physiology
dark urine or pale stools with worsening jaundice
persistent RUQ tenderness with vomiting or inability to tolerate intake
rising cholestatic bloods or concern for obstructed septic patient
firstLineTests: observations and sepsis screen if unwell
ultrasound first-line for gallstones / biliary dilation
MRCP or EUS if duct stone remains suspected after ultrasound
lipase if pancreatitis overlap is plausible

Think about sepsis-pathway escalation, IV access, fluids, and antibiotics in a real clinical pathway when infection is plausible
Urgent senior medical / surgical / GI input if cholangitis or obstructed jaundice is suspected
Think about ERCP or biliary drainage pathways for an obstructed septic picture
escalation: Urgent senior escalation for cholangitis, septic obstruction, worsening jaundice, or diagnostic uncertainty with systemic upset
outputStyle: High-risk hepatobiliary branch with acute septic, obstructive, gallbladder, and chronic cholestatic comparators
